past_medical_history: Bicuspid aortic valve, diagnosed incidentally at age 36 during pre-surgical echocardiogram for knee arthroscopy — described as 'harmless' with no follow-up recommended
Migraine without aura, infrequent episodes (3-4 per year)

Dental extraction of lower right molar 8 weeks ago, complicated by post-procedure bleeding requiring antibiotic prophylaxis (amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 7 days — patient took for only 3 days then stopped because he 'felt fine')
family_history: Father with hypertension, diagnosed at age 52
Mother alive and healthy at age 68
No family history of endocarditis, congenital heart disease, or early cardiovascular events

social_history.smoking: Occasional cigar smoking, approximately 2-3 per month at social events
social_history.alcohol: Moderate drinker, 2-3 beers 3-4 nights per week after gigs
social_history.occupation: Freelance musician (saxophonist), performs at jazz clubs 4-5 nights per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a studio apartment
social_history.exercise: Walks frequently; no formal exercise program
social_history.illicit_drugs: Denies IV drug use. Denies any recreational drug use. Has never used injection drugs.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without aphasia or dysarthria. Attention and concentration intact. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. Follows complex commands without difficulty.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Left central facial droop (forehead sparing). Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Fundoscopic exam: bilateral Roth spots (white-centered retinal hemorrhages) — left eye 2 spots, right eye 1 spot.
neurological_exam.motor: Right side 5/5 throughout. Left arm: deltoid 3/5, biceps 2/5, triceps 2/5, wrist extension 3/5, grip 3/5. Left leg: hip flexion 3/5, knee extension 3+/5, ankle dorsiflexion 3/5. Left pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Left hemisensory deficit to light touch and pinprick involving left arm, leg, and face. Proprioception mildly impaired in left hand. Right side intact to all modalities. Mild left-sided extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper and lower extremity 2+ symmetric. Left upper extremity 3+. Left lower extremity 3+. Left plantar response extensor (Babinski positive). Right plantar flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Right finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal. Left-sided testing limited by weakness.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate safely due to left leg weakness. When assisted to stand, significant left leg buckling.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 12. Breakdown: left facial palsy 1, left arm motor 3, left leg motor 2, sensory 1, extinction/neglect 1, best gaze 0, visual fields 0, language 0, dysarthria 0, limb ataxia unable to assess on left (scored 0 bilaterally per protocol). Additional physical exam findings: splinter hemorrhages in nail beds of 3rd and 4th fingers of right hand and 2nd finger of left hand. Tender erythematous nodules on pads of 2nd and 3rd fingers of left hand (Osler nodes). Non-tender erythematous macules on bilateral palms (Janeway lesions).

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden left arm and leg weakness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Marcus Everett is a 41-year-old right-handed African American man who is a freelance jazz saxophonist, presenting to the emergency department via EMS with acute-onset left-sided weakness beginning approximately 3 hours ago. He was at home preparing for an evening performance when he suddenly noticed his left arm felt heavy and clumsy. Within minutes, his left leg became weak and he was unable to stand without support. His roommate, who was visiting, called 911. On further history, the patient reports that for the past 6 weeks he has been experiencing intermittent low-grade fevers, drenching night sweats, progressive fatigue, poor appetite, and an unintentional 8 kg weight loss. He attributed these symptoms to his demanding performance schedule and a persistent 'flu' that he could not shake. He notes generalized joint aches, particularly in his knees and wrists, that began approximately 4 weeks ago. Two weeks ago he noticed painful red bumps on the pads of his left index and middle fingers, which he thought were from playing saxophone. He also noticed small dark streaks under his fingernails. He has not sought medical attention for these symptoms. He had a dental extraction 8 weeks ago that was complicated by prolonged bleeding, for which he was prescribed amoxicillin for 7 days but took only 3 days of the course. He has a known bicuspid aortic valve discovered 5 years ago, which he was told was benign and required no treatment or follow-up. He explicitly denies any intravenous drug use, past or present. He denies chest pain, frank dyspnea, or lower extremity edema, though he has noticed becoming winded more easily during saxophone performances in the past few weeks. He has had no prior strokes, seizures, or neurological symptoms.

findings.location: Right MCA territory involving right frontal and parietal lobes, primarily cortical and subcortical
findings.size: Approximately 3.5 x 2.8 cm in maximal dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal with restricted diffusion involving right precentral gyrus, postcentral gyrus, and adjacent superior frontal and parietal cortex
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Early subtle hyperintensity in the affected territory
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in right frontoparietal cortex and subcortical white matter

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in right frontoparietal region, no midline shift
findings.borders: Poorly defined, involving both cortical and subcortical tissue in right MCA superior division territory

findings.location: Scattered bilaterally: left parietal cortex (1 focus), left occipital cortex (1 focus), right cerebellar hemisphere (1 focus), left frontal subcortical white matter (1 focus)

signal_characteristics.DWI: Multiple small punctate foci of restricted diffusion in different vascular territories bilaterally

additional_observations: No hemorrhagic transformation in any of the infarcts
No evidence of mycotic aneurysm on the initial MRA of the intracranial circulation
No prior ischemic changes or white matter disease
mri.impression: Acute right MCA territory infarct (right frontoparietal) as the dominant lesion, explaining the patient's left hemiparesis and hemisensory loss. No hemorrhagic transformation. No mycotic aneurysm on initial MRA, though this requires serial monitoring. The multi-territory embolic pattern mandates urgent cardiac evaluation. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 98 bpm, otherwise normal 12-lead electrocardiogram. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes. The sinus tachycardia may reflect underlying infection, pain, or hemodynamic stress.

Cardiac and Other.value: Drawn, pending (4 sets from separate sites)

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 14.8 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 82 % (H), Bands 8 % (H), Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 30.8 % (L), Platelets 128 x10^9/L (L), RBC 3.48 x10^12/L (L), BUN 22 mg/dL (H), Glucose 104 mg/dL (H), ESR 68 mm/hr (H), CRP 92 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 2.4 ng/mL (H), BNP 188 pg/mL (H), Color Dark amber  (H), Protein 1+  (H), RBC 10-20 per HPF (H), Casts Rare RBC casts  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: WBC 14.8 x10^9/L (leukocytosis with left shift, 8% bands)
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (normocytic anemia of chronic disease)
Procalcitonin 2.4 ng/mL (elevated, consistent with bacterial infection)
Urinalysis: proteinuria, microscopic hematuria, rare RBC casts (H)

Blood Cultures.value: Positive at 14 hours: Streptococcus mutans (viridans group streptococci)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aerobic bottle set 1 (right antecubital) Positive at 14 hours: Streptococcus mutans (viridans group streptococci)  (H), Anaerobic bottle set 1 (right antecubital) Positive at 16 hours: Streptococcus mutans  (H), Aerobic bottle set 2 (left antecubital) Positive at 15 hours: Streptococcus mutans  (H), Anaerobic bottle set 2 (left antecubital) Positive at 18 hours: Streptococcus mutans  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: 4/4 blood culture bottles positive for Streptococcus mutans (viridans group streptococci)

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Bicuspid aortic valve with large mobile vegetation, 1.8 cm in maximal dimension, attached to the ventricular surface of the right coronary cusp
No perivalvular abscess identified on TTE

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aortic Valve Bicuspid aortic valve with large mobile vegetation, 1.8 cm in maximal dimension, attached to the ventricular surface of the right coronary cusp  (H), Aortic Regurgitation Moderate qualitative (H), Aortic Stenosis Mild qualitative (H), Pericardium Trivial pericardial effusion  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Large mobile aortic valve vegetation (1.8 cm)

signal_characteristics.CTA: Bilateral ICA, MCA, ACA, vertebral arteries, basilar artery, and PCA are all patent without occlusion or significant stenosis. No thrombus in proximal vessels.

findings.type: No mycotic aneurysm on initial CTA

signal_characteristics.CTA: No focal arterial dilation, outpouching, or irregularity to suggest mycotic aneurysm at this time. Note: mycotic aneurysms may develop over days to weeks and serial imaging is recommended.

output.impression: No large vessel occlusion — the embolic material has fragmented or lodged distally. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Right MCA M2 branch (inferior division), distal to the acute infarct territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: New 4 mm fusiform dilation of a distal right MCA M2 branch not present on the initial study. Irregular vessel wall with focal outpouching. Surrounding FLAIR hyperintensity suggesting inflammation or small perivascular leak.
signal_characteristics.SWI: Susceptibility blooming artifact adjacent to the aneurysm suggesting micro-hemorrhage or hemosiderin

findings.location: Right frontoparietal and scattered bilateral foci

signal_characteristics.DWI: Persistent restricted diffusion in right frontoparietal territory (expected for subacute infarct). Prior small foci show evolution with FLAIR hyperintensity and early DWI normalization.
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Clear FLAIR hyperintensity now defining the full extent of all infarcts

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement, no midline shift

additional_observations: No new areas of restricted diffusion (no new embolic events since treatment initiation)
No hemorrhagic transformation of existing infarcts
The developing mycotic aneurysm warrants close surveillance and possible neurosurgical intervention
output.impression: New 4 mm fusiform dilation of a right MCA M2 branch — highly concerning for developing mycotic aneurysm in the setting of infective endocarditis. Evolution of prior infarcts without hemorrhagic transformation or new embolic events. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Rheumatoid Factor 68 IU/mL (H), C3 Complement 52 mg/dL (L), C4 Complement 8 mg/dL (L), Circulating Immune Complexes Positive  (H), Cryoglobulins Trace positive  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Rheumatoid Factor 68 IU/mL (elevated, common in endocarditis)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to septic embolism from infective endocarditis (Streptococcus mutans, bicuspid aortic valve)

differential.key_distinguishing: Patient is 41 with sinus rhythm on ECG, no AF history; multi-territory bilateral infarcts suggest embolic shower rather than single large vessel occlusion typical of AF

Elevated inflammatory markers and subsequent positive blood cultures point to infection, not primary vasculitis; immunologic findings (low complement, RF) are explained by endocarditis rather than primary autoimmune disease

Positive rheumatoid factor is from endocarditis immune complex formation; antiphospholipid antibodies would need to be specifically tested but the clinical picture (fever, vegetation, positive cultures) points clearly to endocarditis
Multiple bilateral infarcts in different vascular territories cannot be explained by a single vessel dissection; no dissection on CTA; systemic infection signs point to endocarditis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain blood cultures (multiple sets from separate venipuncture sites) BEFORE starting empiric antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: 4/4 bottles positive for Streptococcus mutans (viridans group streptococci)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI to characterize the stroke and identify multi-territory embolic pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right MCA territory infarct plus multiple small bilateral infarcts in different vascular territories — embolic shower pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, cardiac markers, coagulation studies, and urinalysis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, normocytic anemia, elevated ESR/CRP/procalcitonin, proteinuria with microscopic hematuria and RBC casts

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) followed by TEE to identify vegetation on the bicuspid aortic valve
optimal_actions.expected_finding: 1.8 cm mobile vegetation on bicuspid aortic valve with moderate aortic regurgitation

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV antibiotics for endocarditis once blood cultures are drawn (before culture results return)
DO NOT administer IV tPA — endocarditis-related stroke has prohibitively high hemorrhagic transformation risk from septic vasculopathy
Monitor for mycotic aneurysm development with serial vascular imaging (MRA or CTA every 1-2 weeks)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New 4 mm fusiform dilation of right MCA M2 branch on 1-week follow-up — developing mycotic aneurysm

optimal_actions.action: Cardiothoracic surgery consultation for possible early valve replacement given large vegetation (>1.5 cm) with documented cerebral embolization
Avoid anticoagulation in the acute phase due to hemorrhagic transformation risk from septic emboli and possible mycotic aneurysm

critical_actions: Obtain blood cultures (multiple sets) BEFORE starting antibiotics
Order echocardiogram (TTE then TEE if needed) to identify vegetation
Start empiric IV antibiotics for endocarditis once cultures drawn
DO NOT give IV tPA — endocarditis-related stroke has high hemorrhagic transformation risk
Monitor for mycotic aneurysm development with serial vascular imaging
Cardiothoracic surgery consultation for possible valve replacement given large vegetation
contraindicated_actions: IV thrombolysis (high risk of hemorrhagic transformation in septic emboli and potential mycotic aneurysm)
Anticoagulation in the acute phase (risk of hemorrhagic transformation from mycotic aneurysm and septic vasculopathy)
Discharging without identifying the embolic source in a patient with multi-territory strokes
Attributing febrile illness to 'flu' without blood cultures in a patient with known valvular disease and acute stroke
key_reasoning_points: Multiple acute infarcts in DIFFERENT vascular territories (bilateral hemispheres + cerebellum) strongly suggest an embolic shower from a proximal source — this pattern should immediately trigger cardiac evaluation

Subacute constitutional symptoms (6 weeks of fever, weight loss, night sweats, arthralgias) combined with acute stroke in a patient with known valvular disease must raise suspicion for infective endocarditis
Wide pulse pressure (142/48) is a key physical exam finding indicating aortic regurgitation — in the context of a bicuspid valve and fever, this suggests valve destruction from endocarditis

Peripheral stigmata of endocarditis are present: Osler nodes (painful finger nodules from immune complex vasculitis), Janeway lesions (painless palm macules from septic emboli), Roth spots (white-centered retinal hemorrhages from immune complex deposition), and splinter hemorrhages
Known bicuspid aortic valve is a major predisposing factor for endocarditis (20x increased risk compared to normal valve)
Recent dental extraction with incomplete antibiotic course (3 of 7 days) is the likely portal of entry for Streptococcus mutans, an oral commensal organism

IV tPA is contraindicated in endocarditis-related stroke due to hemorrhagic risk from septic vasculopathy, potential mycotic aneurysms, and hemorrhagic transformation rates exceeding 20% in case series

red_herrings.data_point: Patient explicitly denies IV drug use
red_herrings.intended_effect: May lead clinician to dismiss endocarditis as a diagnosis, since IVDU is the most commonly associated risk factor in public perception
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Non-IVDU endocarditis is common and accounts for the majority of endocarditis cases, especially with predisposing native valve disease (bicuspid aortic valve) and a clear portal of entry (dental procedure)

red_herrings.data_point: Positive rheumatoid factor on immunology panel
red_herrings.intended_effect: May suggest a primary rheumatologic or autoimmune etiology, potentially leading to misdiagnosis of vasculitis or connective tissue disease
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Rheumatoid factor is elevated in up to 50% of subacute bacterial endocarditis cases due to chronic immune stimulation; it is not specific for rheumatoid arthritis and should be interpreted in clinical context

red_herrings.intended_effect: May be considered a platelet-related or NSAID-related risk factor for stroke
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Irrelevant to the embolic mechanism; ibuprofen does not cause cardioembolic stroke; the stroke is from endocarditis-related septic embolism

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — 41-year-old musician with subacute constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats) now presenting with acute MCA stroke. Multi-territory infarcts on MRI reveal an embolic shower. The underlying diagnosis is infective endocarditis on a bicuspid aortic valve (Streptococcus mutans from incomplete dental antibiotic prophylaxis). Requires recognizing systemic signs of endocarditis, obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics, avoiding thrombolysis, and monitoring for mycotic aneurysm.
clinical_pearls: Multi-territory bilateral brain infarcts on DWI MRI is the imaging hallmark of endocarditis-related embolic shower and should trigger immediate cardiac evaluation
IV tPA is contraindicated in endocarditis-related stroke — hemorrhagic transformation rates from septic vasculopathy and mycotic aneurysm rupture approach 20-40% in published case series
Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital cardiac anomaly (1-2% of the population) and carries a 20x increased risk of infective endocarditis

The modified Duke criteria require a combination of major and minor criteria: positive blood cultures with typical organism + echocardiographic evidence constitute 2 major criteria (definite endocarditis)
Mycotic aneurysms develop in 2-10% of endocarditis cases with CNS embolization and may not be present on initial imaging — serial MRA/CTA surveillance is essential
